role_summary: Saint Augustine, Florida beautician and civil rights organizer; co-organizer with Dr. Robert Bagner Hayling (catalog entry 103) of the early-1960s St. Augustine civil rights protests; participant in the kneel-ins at segregated St. Augustine churches; fundraiser for the St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument commemorating the local civil rights movement.
ai_reading: Vickers's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 78), with Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan (entry 51) as the top match and the broader cluster including the joint Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer interview (entry 4), Purcell Maurice Conway (entry 99), and Charles McLaurin (entry 17). The Robert Bagner Hayling cosine connection is the catalog's clearest St. Augustine civil-rights-organizing cosine: both Vickers and Hayling co-organized the same Florida-east-coast civil-rights direct-action campaign that drew Martin Luther King Jr. to St. Augustine in spring 1964 (the campaign that helped produce the Civil Rights Act of 1964); the embedding catches that shared St. Augustine organizing register in both their testimonies. The novel observation her page surfaces is the small-business-owner-as-organizer signature: Vickers's beauty shop was an organizing-infrastructure node, the same pattern Peggy Jean Connor's beauty shop in Hattiesburg occupies in entry 96.
biographical_paragraph: While Vickers's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), her external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid and the city of St. Augustine's civil-rights archive [src: 1, src: 2]. Vickers was born in 1923 in St. Augustine, Florida and attended Excelsior High School in the segregated St. Augustine school system. During World War II she worked at a New York shipyard, returning to St. Augustine with her husband after the war and working as a beautician [src: 1]. She co-organized civil rights protests in St. Augustine alongside her neighbor Dr. Robert Bagner Hayling (catalog entry 103, the NAACP Youth Council adviser and Florida SCLC president whose 1964 organizing brought Martin Luther King Jr. to the city) and participated in kneel-ins at segregated St. Augustine churches across the early 1960s [src: 1]. She later raised money to build the St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument, the city's memorial to the local civil rights movement [src: 2].
movement_context: The St. Augustine campaign that Vickers and Hayling co-organized was the SCLC's last major direct-action campaign before the July 2, 1964 signing of the Civil Rights Act, and the historiographical record credits the St. Augustine campaign with helping break the Senate filibuster that had held the bill in committee through the spring of 1964 [src: 3]. The campaign's national-attention moments included the June 11, 1964 arrest of Martin Luther King Jr. and Ralph Abernathy (catalog page available) at the segregated Monson Motor Lodge restaurant on the St. Augustine bayfront, and the June 18, 1964 "swim-in" at the same property when Monson Motor Lodge owner James Brock poured muriatic acid into the swimming pool while integrated swimmers (an action photographed by the Associated Press's Horace Cort and reproduced in the historiographical record as the single image most credited with shifting Senate Republican Minority Leader Everett Dirksen's position on cloture) [src: 3, 4]. The kneel-in actions Vickers participated in were a Florida-east-coast extension of the SCLC tactical pattern developed in the late-1950s Albany, Georgia and 1962-1963 Birmingham, Alabama campaigns, in which sustained low-violence direct-action presence at segregated church and commercial facilities created the public-photograph-as-tactical-object documentary record that shaped national congressional opinion on pending civil rights legislation [src: 4].
legacy_and_reception: The St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument Vickers raised funds for was dedicated May 14, 2011 in the city's Plaza de la Constitucion (the same plaza where the 1964 demonstrations had been held), with thirty-one foot soldiers' names inscribed on the central plaque and Vickers herself among the surviving organizers attending the dedication [src: 2]. Her oral history was recorded by interviewer David P. Cline on April 16, 2014 in St. Augustine; the entry carries a publication-block tier in the CRHP audit substrate because of unresolved internal-transcript issues flagged in Pass 1 through Pass 8 of the audit cascade [src: 1, 5]. The publication-block designation does not impair the externally-documented record of her organizing role; it constrains direct quotation of the transcript content itself until the audit issues are resolved [src: 5].

gallery.caption: St. Augustine civil rights demonstrations, 1964, from the Florida Memory project of the State Library and Archives of Florida. The footage documents demonstrators at Butler Beach, integrated occupation of city streets, and intervention by the Florida Highway Patrol during the spring 1964 SCLC direct-action campaign Vickers co-organized.
gallery.photographer: State Library and Archives of Florida (Florida Memory project)

gallery.alt: St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument plaque, dedicated May 14, 2011
gallery.caption: The St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument plaque in the Plaza de la Constitucion, dedicated May 14, 2011. Vickers led the fundraising drive for the monument; thirty-one foot soldiers' names are inscribed on the central plaque, commemorating participants in the 1960s civil rights protests in St. Augustine.
